Not sure how i got this to load since I am not a subscriber, but current Vinous report on Santa Cruz Mtns says this,

“ 2020: A Question Mark

Readers will recall that the 2020 wildfires were especially damaging in the Santa Cruz Mountains. “Everything was so smoked in, you couldn’t even tell what was good and what was bad,” Tommy Fogarty explained. Fogarty will not release any of his 2020 red wines, but had not decided the fate of the 2020 whites as we went to press. It’s a similar story at Rhys, which will release only appellation-level wines from the Santa Cruz Mountains, although there will be single-vineyard bottlings from Anderson Valley and the new Mt. Pajaro property. Even before the fires, though, 2020 was an exceptionally dry and warm year. Aside from obvious smoke taint, intense smoke also blocks light, which is a critical ingredient in the final phase of ripening. The first 2020s I have tasted point to an extremely challenging vintage, but we will see.
”

That sounds rather far-fetched to me. Ridge has been cautioning about the low production of the '21 MB since early this year. The low yields were mentioned more generally in last December’s harvest report:

“While yields were down for all the Bordeaux varieties, cabernet was a full third below average. Also notable and another effect of the drought, was the very small size of the berries. This resulted in wines with tremendous concentration but posed a challenge in managing the level of tannin of each fermentation. As the year comes to an end, forty-five lots are slowly completing malolactic fermentation and we expect all to finish in January.”

If there’s a serious issue with the '20 MB I’m confident Ridge will do the right thing for those of us who did purchase it on futures. I don’t see them exacerbating the problem of an already short '21 MB release by diverting some of that limited production though.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
K_Kladder:

Thanks for additional info. I can’t speak to it based on personal experience, but others here have stated before that smoke taint tastes like a dirty ashtray. Personally I’ve only had 1 2020 and it was fantastic. Not questioning your experience, just trying to understand .

There are quite a few compounds that can come from smoke and affect a wine. The most commonly tested for is guaiacol, which is barrels and some grape varieties such as Syrah. So, a moderate introduction of that might not be noticed. Above that, it’s smoky, like we intentionally introduce to some foods. A bit odd in wine, but some people like it and it can have context to consume it, like rauchbier does. Some taint shows peaty, like the smoke tainted style of Scotch that’s unfortunately popular. Weird to have in wine. Dirty ashtray is pretty bad, though some people don’t notice it at lower thresholds. When it’s bad, for me it builds up on the palate with each sip and takes a long time to fade. There’s also a gritty texture on the finish that accompanies that. A type of bitterness is also common. Muted character is another thing some people note, which might be a byproduct of treating for taint.

There’s a big gap between Skyline ridge and the top of Black Mtn. where Ridge is. The fire went up the west side, ruining the crops from the Rhys, Mindego and Fogarty vineyards there, and blew across. The upper Monte Bello vineyards weren’t picked. The question is if smoke settled down to their various lower plots to a significant degree. The really bad taint compounds are dense and drop out of the air over time, so the air can look and smell smoky, but not affect a wine.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked:
We had a .375 - opened it around 3 and tasted it through 11 pm, leaving about a half-glass for tomorrow. Great, deep typical Ridge cab aroma when the cork pops, way too tight (duh!), some cranberry, but not too tannic. Over the course of the evening, it spread its wings and became relatively approachable. Once or twice I thought I detected some bitterness in the tannins but they seemed to smoothe out. I didn’t tell my wife why we were having this, just that I wanted her opinion on how it tasted, if she noticed anything unusual. Other than it being stupidly young, no, she didn’t. So far: whew, no smoke as far as I can tell - it seems like a success. We’ll get more info tomorrow.

Now here’s my issue with them saying nothing about the fires. I feel that anyone who spends $200 on a bottle of wine knows California was on fire in 2020, and that many other producers had issues, and that Ridge got its share of smoke. I don’t think I would have cracked this if I’d heard from them that there was nothing to worry about. I have so far really appreciated the producers who have acknowledged the issue and explained that there isn’t going to be an issue. 2020 Di Co is delicious. Myriad’s Napa Valley cab is delicious. Why make us wonder why you aren’t talking about the elephant in the room? I don’t know.

Anyway, so far so good. I’ll report more tomorrow.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Larry_Stein:

I’ve mentioned this before, but I feel it bears repeating. As background, I poured in the MB tasting room for the year that '11 MB was available as a bonus pour. All bottles of all the wines are tasted prior to the start of the day just in case any aren’t sound. I tasted MANY bottles of the '11. There is REAL bottle variation with that wine.

But, it that REAL bottle variation? Very young wines go through phases. If every bottle was in a bad phase, you’d just say it was “closed” and be done with it. In an “at home” situation opening a single bottle of some classic wine that early, we have terms like “cradle robbing” and would easily chalk it up to being closed. But, getting a bigger picture and cradle robbing bottles from the same case and having them show dramatically different to each other, and you draw a different conclusion.

Winemakers deal with this by letting wines rest after bottling. On a normal level, some wines are good to go right away, others need an extended time to reliably stabilize or recover from bottling. It’s usually predictable, but odd things happen.

So, I think of this as “bottle mood” rather than bottle variation, as the wine in those bottles are cycling through the same phases, but aren’t in sync with each other.

If you want an insider Ridge story on this, it’s with the '94 Monte Bello. When they were looking at doing a library release, the bottles were showing a lot of such variation. They check in again at whatever interval. Some years later the bottles were all showing consistently, so they did the library release. posts.name: Michael Kanbergs
posts.cooked:
I think the convo upthread about taint being binary is a sorta interesting one and pretty relevant to this thread. First, I do believe “flaws” are binary, in that they are either present or they aren’t. But I also think there are gradations - duh - in the intensity of the “flaw”, and definite differences in how they’re individually perceived. I think Ridge knows what they’re doing, business-wise, and most folks will very much enjoy this wine, with a small percentage asking for a refund. If you perceive smoke taint in this wine, you are - in my opinion, and as a retailer myself - very much entitled to a refund.

But I think the convo around “flaws” itself being binary is a bit of a bummer, both professionally and personally. I like the Grateful Dead. I’d argue there are more “flawed” shows than perfect shows in their history, but I think those “flaws” are part of the thing that drives the intrigue and mystique of the band, and make for a more complex listening experience. When Jerry loses his voice and there’s a whole show of Bobby songs it isn’t exactly my favorite, and I won’t return to that show all that much, but I’m really glad it exists because it only adds to the galaxy of my experience as a Deadhead.

I have a couple bottles of 2020 Monte Bello. I won’t be in a hurry to drink them, and I’ll probably open another bottle or two on the nights I open those bottles. But I’m really happy I have those bottles and I won’t be returning them or asking for a refund - I bought them direct, not wholesale - no matter how they’re affected by smoke. Cork taint is another story, because that has nothing to do with the wine, but smoke taint - to me - stands apart as a “flaw” because it’s vintage-derived. It’s an inherent part of the wine, its story, and the winery’s experience that year. I’m very interested to learn how the '20 MB fits in with the 19 and '21 vintages in a mature vertical several years down the line. That experience is far more interesting to me as a Deadhead - er, wine lover - than a perfect bottle of wine on its own, and vastly more interesting than a missing vintage in a vertical.

posts.name: Tom Gutting
posts.cooked: I don’t know if you can really treat smoke taint as a feature rather than a flaw. It is vintage-specific as a result of the environment at the time, but fundamentally, it’s an unwanted intrusion into the wine – like frost damage would be or brett. I agree there’s a distinction between what we might call “external” factors (e.g., cork taint) that flaws a wine and “internal” or “vintage” factors (fires that cause smoke taint, frost, etc.), but I’d say those are two items contained in the same bucket of “flaw” that have an impact on the wine and whether it’s drinkable. Not to say, of course, there also aren’t degrees of the flaw and individual sensitivities to it.

Not to say, of course, there also aren’t degrees of the flaw and individual sensitivities to it.

Great points - and one other thing to note about ‘flaws’ like TCA (and smoke taint) - we are all going to have different sensitivities to all aromatic compounds, and we’ve all been there when someone else finds a wine corked but the majority of the others at the table do not.

The bottom line is that it is a ‘fault’ - but one that many will not be able to recognize or even care that it’s there. Should a winery knowingly release a wine that has a low level of a taint? That’s up to each and every winery to decide. Should said winery be willing to take back these products if someone finds them ‘faulty’? Hell yeah . . .

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
I’ve had a number of smoke tainted wines, mainly from 2008, though a few others here and there. My experience is that the impact is highly dependent on the grape variety, and of course the amount of exposure and resulting taint. Pinot does not hold up well, while syrah can, and strangely may even benefit if it’s not too strong (which goes along with my theory that some bacon/meat/truffle notes in syrah may be brett, and not some innate character of the fruit). I don’t think I’ve ever had a cabernet based wine that was suspect of smoke taint.

Part of my point is that I’m not convinced there is some telltale signature on the palate that signifies smoke taint, especially if it’s not detectable on the nose. But that may also depend on how close the fire is, and how long the exposure was.

posts.name: Chris Weishaar
posts.cooked:
We had our Friday night brown bagger last night and since we needed an extra bottle to round out the flights I used it as an opportunity to serve this double blind to the group - all of whom are experienced tasters and quite familiar with Monte Bello as well. I knew this bottle was in the second flight, but I didn’t know where until we began unveiling the bottles.

Decanted 5.75 hours, served in the last flight of reds 3.75 hours later, about 9.5 hours after the cork was first pulled.

I poured a small one ounce taste when first opened- slightly grapey, also kirsch, red plum and blue fruit. Hint of something floral underneath, a bit like carnation blossoms. Something a bit woodsy or stemmy. Full-bodied. Tannins are firm, dense. A touch austere. Black and red currant, red plum, black cherry. Blue fruit finish.

And then later during the tasting- teriyaki, soy and black fruit. Fine tannins, with teriyaki and dark fruit flavors. Good length in the finish.

I did not find the gritty quality some have mentioned as an indication of smoke taint, but this was like no other young Monte Bello I have ever had, and I’ve had a few. The rest of the group, again tasting completely blind, agreed- atypical and certainly not what one expects from Monte Bello. Aside from that sentiment, no one suggested smoke taint or found an obvious flaw. For me, the “teriyaki” note certainly has a smoky connotation to it, and at the time I wrote the note I did not know I was tasting the MB.

I have two mags and five bottles and may reach out about returning a mag and a couple of the 750s. In the end, I may just leave it alone and drink them all on the early side and enjoy what I can, chalking things up to the hardships of the vintage.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked: This might be repeating other people’s comments, but if so, it bears repeating. Somewhere around 20-25% of people cannot taste or smell smoke taint, or at least are sufficiently insensitive to it that they cannot smell or taste it at levels likely to be found in commercial wine. And for the remaining 75-80% of people, sensitivity varies enormously. So bear that in mind as you taste these wines and read other people’s comments. I’ve been in a number of tastings where a wine is clearly smoke tainted but some people don’t notice a problem. I’ve also tasted alongside a winemaker with a savant-like sensitivity to smoke taint who could pick it out in wines that tasted normal to me.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
DanielP:

Don’t forget when they reoffer those same wines back to their mailing list members after release.

Yeah, that’s pretty awkward. Of course their mailing list is much more than just MB Collectors. It’s understandable they need to allocate the wines to different commitments. They need to maintain distribution relationships, so can’t just say “no dice” there. They need to have the wine available for purchase at the winery over a good period of time. Things like that, to keep awareness. But, the question is how to cut back with a low production without harming their goodwill.

Club members are locking in sales, evangelists for the brand. They protect long-time customers from the cut, so seem to realize something there. But, they do have a history of hair-brained decisions. They seem to be under-valuing new customers, who surely are more fickle statistically. For some context, they’ve previously grandfathered in old, higher allocations, while limiting the amount of MB new members can buy at futures price. And, if an existing member cuts their allocation, they can’t then increase back above the limit. They knew with the boom in market awareness and price, members were getting an incredible deal. They also increased the member price quite a bit.

Of course, we can all see the raw direct sales numbers for them sell at full price rather than club price look much better. But, again, that doesn’t consider goodwill. I think there’s a lot they could do, from where they are, to balance things better. Offering a pair of bottles to newer members should be a very high priority. Having MB available to purchase direct on release is one thing. Having so much that they’re actively advertising it is another. Seems like a major screw up to me.

I wonder how many cases this denial to new members represents, and where that fits in context. It probably is a large enough number that they didn’t feel they could just eat it. But, I’m certain they could’ve done a much better job, in may ways.

posts.name: Thomas DeBiase
posts.cooked:
Tyler-

What you’re describing… is winemaking dude. Every year we have to dodge vectors and steer the wines to the best representation of what they can be from the piece of dirt that they’re grown. Those vectors can be mold, mildew, a whole host of rots, damage from migratory or nesting birds, turkeys, wild pigs, wind, hail, heat spikes, and even smoke. So every single year, we deal with any number of those issues and like you said, often have to omit blocks or sections of vineyards or cull barrels from a program. That’s why we put the vintage on the label. The 2020 vintage saw smoke, lots of it. I’ve had the 2020 Ridge Montebello, lots of it. I haven’t detected any smoke in it, and it tastes like Montebello. You may perceive it as a lesser young Montebello, and you are correct… others may perceive it as a superior young Montebello and they are… also correct!! We’re all the expert at what we like and shouldn’t have to rely on others to judge for us. Ridge has displayed nothing but integrity over 50+ years so I have no reason to doubt them now.

Can we also mention in this thread that Ridge farms more acres CCOF Organic than ANY other wine organization in Sonoma County. They’re walking the walk. Doing things the RIGHT WAY for their customers.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
I’ll restate my impression from last March. Also, that Ridge is my benchmark winery, with the Mento Bello tasting room one of the closest to my house. I’ve been going to the futures tastings, with barrel samples, new releases and library wines. Being a fan and having so many local wines geeks who are fans, including several who’ve been Ridge customers since the '60s, I’ve tasted every commercial Monte Bello, including the three vintage blends, except the '62, '71 and '72. (The '62 was the world’s best smelling vinegar by a million miles, and I have a '71. Oh, I sort of had '72, which I brought to a vertical tasting. When the capsule came off, the cork bubbled. Sad.)

The '20 was excellent. We were marveling, Tres among us. But, I got the tell tale ashtray and grit on the finish. Not much, just enough I’d want it with food. The Estate Merlot and Cab both had this, too. People were going gaga and debating which they preferred. (To me, most of the estate Merlot is meh, but the lots that always make Monte Bello are stunning, world class.)

A couple months later I had the Estate Cab. Bold and vibrant, very impressive. Except this smoke and ash crept up, building on the palate to the point it was off-putting and I had to dump. I retasted from another bottle, maybe 20 minutes later with a friend. Same. *But, no one else was getting that or less than impressed.

So, it’s my experience with Monte Bello through very young phases to fully mature (and a bit beyond, with some ‘60s vintages), as well as my winemaking experience, that I wish I’d tasted that bottle alongside Larry and the others. I guess this topic will make a version of that inevitable. Right now I’d expect the wine to be shut down. Sounds like it’ “moody”, with some bottles shut down and some open for business. But, I think I can dissect whatever issues are going on instead of some sort of post hoc, ergo propter hoc conclusion. (I’m also fine with being inconclusive, and prefer that to undue certainty.)

The offer was to people who’ve reached out to them. You can search far upthread for that. They stand behind the wine and will take it back. You don’t have to open a bottle and taste it. None of the lab testing they did detected taint. But, I noted upthread we’re only learning all of the smoke taint compounds and precursor compounds. They wouldn’t have known all of what to test for. Old-school testing was just for guaiacol, which also comes from oak and some grape varieties. A marginal increase isn’t necessarily. But, that’s what was the signature of smoke tainted '08 Sonoma Pinots, literal smokey taste. You can buy liquid smoke for culinary use and plenty of foods that use it. I think that’s what a lot of people are looking for and not finding in the '20 Monte Bello. I detected none, but got it in the later tasting of the '20 Estate Cab.

There were some '08 Sonoma Pinots that became terrible early on. I’ve also had some mature ones, both in 10 year retrospective tastings and individual double-blind, that had obvious smoke, but not too much, and were still excellent. A minority of tasters arguing they weren’t tainted, thinking it was from the barrel. The most amusing was the one I called the vintage and region because of the smoke taint, and someone still disputed it being smoke tainted.

Posting a note from CT’er @ BDISK1235 who has written a thoughtful note on this wine. "I struggled to write this note. Opened and decanted and drank over the course of 12 hours. Tight and structured, with big fruit and tannins initially. It is evident that the fruit is excellent. Over time it mellowed and opened up a bit. The wine is well made, but something was off. I tasted with my wife (who is not aware of the 2020 issues and has a better palate than me) and she noticed it as well. At the outset, the wine had a gritty taste (if that makes sense) that hit in the back of my lower jaw. It also had a teriyaki/soy flavor that I’ve never experienced with any other wine. Over the course of the day, those flavors seemed to integrate a bit more, and to me there was the faintest hint of smoke in the background. Not pronounced like other smoke tainted wines I have had that taste like an ash tray, but lurking in the background as if you drank it after smoking a cigarette the day before.

Ultimately, I don’t think the above doomed the wine - it was pleasant to drink and the qualities I noted were not overbearing or overwhelming. However, given what I tasted now, I wouldn’t want to set bottles down for the long haul given that they are not like other young MB’s and I don’t know how the flavors I noticed will affect the wine in the long run.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Inspired by this thread, Ed Kurtzman decided to open one at his Super Bowl party. I did get a touch of smoke in the mix on the nose, but it’s not prominent and most people wouldn’t notice. Pretty nose with a good dose of Draper perfume. That palate was thin and disjointed. Again a bit of grit, bitter, ash tray in the mix, but not to the level most people notice. But, not great.

Part two of the experiment was to see how the remaining half bottle performed the next day. I accepted that assignment, bringing it to a Berserker Chinese New Year feast. The nose was bolder, attractive, making the smoke near impossible to detect. The weight had picked up, with bolder darker baby fruit, but still light bodied. That diminished the taint aspects. The best comment likened it to a young Beaujolais. The fruit character was different, of course, but not quite Cabby either. Clearly a Ridge wine. Maybe what declassified Geyserville would taste like. Blind I’d be thinking this was made from predominently thin-skinned grapes. I’d guess they picked on the early side and pressed earlier or excluded late press. There’s just not the depth of complexity or oomph of stuffing. No confidence it will be long lived or improve much. From the day two, it should be better in a few years or benefit from a decant. It does not resemble any previous vintage I’ve had, including the crappy '98, which eventually came together to being good, just good. Right now this is a decent Fleurie with Draper perfume and 2% Petite Sirah that you’d be happy paying $30 for. I’ll hold out the possibility varietal character and terroir will show up, and maybe some more weight. No confidence in that, though.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
T_Melloni:

Having had some 2008 Anderson Valley pinots (Anthill Farms anyone?) I have had experience with smoke taint. I didn’t find those traits in the 2020 MB.

Smoke taint is any or all of dozens of compounds that can make it into grape tissue via smoke. Lots of things burn. The crest of the Montebello ridge is directly across from the Santa Cruz Mountains ridgeline. You can see Rhys from Ridge. All of those vineyards - Rhys’ Alpine, Horseshoe, Skyline, the Fogarty estate, Mindego Ridge - where all written off. Ridge wrote off the clearly effected blocks. They then tested for the smoke taint compound you are familiar with (and presumably others) and discarded those lots. So, of course you didn’t experience the smoke taint you got in those '08s. They thought they covered their bases. The wine did not taste mediocre at their Spring Release event last year, either. But, the wine is smoke tainted.

posts.name: Michael Kanbergs
posts.cooked:
This is such an interesting post in light of the accusations upthread of the wine being “clearly tainted.” I have limited experience - we all do, really - with smoke-tainted wines, but I own a wine shop in Portland, OR and tasted well over 100 examples of '20 WV Pinot. I also caught the '08 Anderson Valley wines as a buyer in California. In my experience, a “clearly [smoke] tainted” wine behaves much like a clearly cork tainted wine: without some type of mediation, it doesn’t improve. The flaws only become more accentuated as the wine is exposed to air. Full stop.

This isn’t at all a knock against you, Todd - I think your rhetoric has been very even handed, actually; you just happened to post the follow-up - but I really think people should take a moment to pause and turn toward humility instead of authority. If the wine improves at all on the second or third day, we really don’t have any idea how the wine will age, and what’s in the bottle 15-20 years from now will likely be more interesting, even as a data point, then the $$ you’ll get back from returning it. That’s my opinion, of course, and your money and wine collection is your own - our views on value being sundry if not legion - but some of the conclusions ITT seem fairly rash and motivated more by the desire to be right first than a desire to find the actual truth of the matter.

Interesting question, and one we ponder a lot. When does a fault become a flaw, and how much should faults and flaws be penalized on a numerical scale?

Haut Brion 1990, for example, contains around 6,000 ppm of volatile phenols (the aromatic signature of brettanomyces). The threshold of perception is supposedly around 300 ppm, though in reality a lot lower if you are trained. How should one score the 1990 Haut Brion?

The reality is that the 2020 Monte Bello remains a lot more pleasurable to drink than most wines rated 84/100…

posts.name: Mike Officer
posts.cooked:
K Prime in Santa Rosa was testing for 10 smoke related compounds at least as early as August 2020. It’s possible Ridge used them for at least some lots in addition to or in lieu of ETS since ETS got so backed up. But there are two issues with all the testing done back then. One, we didn’t have baselines for these compounds in a non-smoke tainted year. Some of the compounds tested for could be present in some/all grape varieties every year. Two, research indicates now that we may have been testing for the wrong compounds. Volatile phenols alone do not create smoke taint in wine. It’s the combination of volatile phenols with thiophenols.

Smoke taint is incredibly complicated and still not well understood. Ultimately, at this point it simply comes down to how the wine smells and taste (keeping in mind that a huge percentage of people can’t even detect smoke taint).

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Alright. It’s a morass. Maybe I can provide some helpful context to go with the excellent comments from other ITB folks. The main point I’d like to try to drive home is that analytics are VERY difficult to interpret, especially in the absence of context.

I’ve personally conducted GCMS and LCMS analyses for smoke markers in the past, both free and bound (glycosides), and I’ve spoken with analysts and wine chemists much more experienced than I, and what I will say is that there are not many people who are well equipped to interpret the results of smoke analysis. Different labs can get quite different results from one another, and the old method used for bound markers (acid hydrolysis + GCMS) gives very different numbers to the now-popular direct analysis by LCMS. So you have to know what you’re comparing to.

It is also extremely difficult to interpret smoke data without baseline information. So-called “smoke compounds” are found naturally in grapes to an extent that varies by variety, location, and the type of smoke compound. It is very possible for an external observer to see a smoke analysis and to think a wine is tainted when it never saw any smoke at all, and even in the absence of oak, which also adds smoke compounds to wines due to the toasting of barrels - albeit typically in a different ratio to that produced by wildfire. But then wildfire smoke compound ratios also vary by location, distance from the fire to the vine, and fuel source.

It looks like TWA went further than most would, so I do give kudos to them for that. But unless they had access to Ridge’s baseline data (if that exists) then the most they could do would likely be to compare their results to a Santa Cruz Mountains Cab-heavy Bordeaux blend baseline, which ETS may be able to provide - I don’t recall how specific their public comparison database gets. To be fair to TWA, four times higher than 99th percentile doesn’t seem great…especially when the most important thing has already happened: an experienced taster has identified that the wine is smoke tainted. As Mike Officer and others here have pointed out, that’s what matters. I just wince a little when people lean heavily on the analytical data to support their outrage because it’s usually more complicated.

To answer the question regarding thiophenol analyses, I wouldn’t be surprised if that is eventually offered by commercial labs, but it is a PITA to analyse quantitatively because we’re talking a thousand times lower concentrations than existing smoke markers and extreme oxygen sensitivity.

James_Mc:

Would it be fair to say a “baseline” could be a vintage when there wasnt threat of smoke, say 2018 or 2019?

Yes. Many wineries have analyzed their 2021/2/3 wines so that they have a baseline for the future. I would imagine insurance claims from future fires could involve these data as well as supporting picking or treatment decisions, though I am by no means an expert in the insurance matter.

Alan_Rath:

Not sure Monte Bello has ever gone through a significant fire season before this, so they probably don’t have baseline data. Wouldn’t you want that data from either unfermented juice, or wine before it goes into barrel?

As someone else commented, they could certainly do comparisons today of 2020 bottles vs. recent years with no fires

For sure, and especially unlikely that they’d have the bound marker data. But yes, presumably Ridge would have had access to at least the 2021 and possibly 2022 data before making release decisions. As you say, these data are best taken from wine immediately post-ferment before they see any oak. They could also have taken back vintages of bottled wine from non-impacted years and analyzed those to provide a profile of what these markers generally look like in finished wines, and what the vintage to vintage variability looks like. Maybe they did do this, and maybe they thought the results from 2020 were within an acceptable range based on historical numbers. Depending on how you interpret the results you could get pretty different conclusions. If you just summed all the free markers or just guaiacol and 4-methyl guaiacol that could lead you to a different decision than, say, summing total cresols. And I’ve heard from people more familiar than I that syringol doesn’t correlate well with smoke impact.

larry_schaffer:

One other intersection point - the WA reviewer only tried and tested a single bottle. Could there be variability here? Just a thought . . .

There is a thought I’ve heard in the wine chemistry community that because thiophenols are redox-sensitive, you could observe bottle variation in the sensory expression of smoke taint due to different amounts of oxygen transmission through the closure. I have not seen any demonstration of this but it’s an idea.

posts.name: Mike Officer
posts.cooked:
Alan_Rath:

Not sure Monte Bello has ever gone through a significant fire season before this, so they probably don’t have baseline data. Wouldn’t you want that data from either unfermented juice, or wine before it goes into barrel?

As someone else commented, they could certainly do comparisons today of 2020 bottles vs. recent years with no fires.

Ben made many great points. But regarding a baseline, keep in mind that we may not even be analyzing for the right compounds. Researchers in Oregon tried to create an organoleptically smoke tainted wine by taking a clean wine and adding back volatile phenols (guaiacol, cresol, phenol, eugenol, etc) but were unsuccessful. It wasn’t until they added thiophenols to the base wine that they got a smoke tainted wine. But we don’t have the ability yet to analyze for thiophenols. So how helpful is baseline data for volatile phenols? At best it’s part of the puzzle but without thiophenol baseline data, we’re still in the dark.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
M_Officer:

But regarding a baseline, keep in mind that we may not even be analyzing for the right compounds. Researchers in Oregon tried to create an organoleptically smoke tainted wine by taking a clean wine and adding back volatile phenols (guaiacol, cresol, phenol, eugenol, etc) but were unsuccessful. It wasn’t until they added thiophenols to the base wine that they got a smoke tainted wine. But we don’t have the ability yet to analyze for thiophenols. So how helpful is baseline data for volatile phenols?

I think this is an important question and, while I generally usher people away from viewing analytical data on marker compounds as objective truths, I know that there is value in current marker compounds and in getting baseline data on them. A few reasons for that:

  1. Free volatile phenols, even if they do not cause all of the sensory impacts of smoke, are elevated in all smoke-impacted wines. In this sense, labs try to emphasize that these are markers and not necessarily the offending molecules themselves. They are pretty easy and quick to measure by GCMS and don’t require any fancy preparation.
  2. Baseline data may vary from vineyard to vineyard, but is pretty consistent in a given variety in a given vineyard from year to year. If you see smoke impact, it’ll show up in the smoke markers. Exactly how impacted the wine is will have to be confirmed by sensory analysis, but if you analyse enough wines alongside sensory impact, as places like ETS and AWRI have as well as some larger wine companies, you see usable trends that can be used to make informed decisions in the absence of thiophenol data.
  3. Several other sensory issues in wine have similar analytical contexts regarding marker compounds. Reduction is a good example. Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) levels are always elevated in wines that smell awful because of reduction problems, but it’s not always the hydrogen sulfide itself that is the main offender, so looking at something with 3 ppb vs 30 ppb H2S, you can’t necessarily say which is more unpleasant smelling! But H2S can be measured quickly and easily without fancy equipment or trained analysts, which makes it a valuable marker as well as an offending molecule.

P.S. The volatile phenols used as marker compounds do contribute to the aroma of smoke taint at high levels. Back in 2020 some wineries with vineyards immediately next to the flames showed me ferments that basically smelled like barbecue sauce. And if you spike a wine with the amount of guaiacol some of those samples had, it does smell like smoke. So it’s not doing nothing. Thiophenols are so pungent at such low concentrations that you could spike a wine with thiophenols to give it “smoke taint” but you could be spiking it with way more than would ever be in a natural smoke-impacted wine. Until we have more widespread reliable thiophenol analysis it’ll be hard to interpret those data.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
CF_Binnig:

I think of testing as producing objectively verifiable, reproducible results. How did Ridge not get the same results from its sample testing, that WA obtained from the sample it sent to ETS?

This was discussed some upthread, but three main reasons it’s not so clear-cut:

  1. 2020 was a crazy year and people were sending to any labs that would take them. ETS had an enormous backlog despite running a huge number of instruments 24/7 for months because everyone needed analyses all at the same time. So there was variability in the data available to winemakers, and Interlaboratory variability in smoke measurements can be quite high depending on the methods used.

  2. Ridge was interpreting data likely in the context of other data on its other wines, possibly including Monte Bello from non-smoke years, which TWA most probably wasn’t. So even if the numbers were the same, the interpretative context was not. This goes back to the importance of baseline data.

  3. A full smoke panel gives you concentrations of 10-12 different compounds, and deciding which ones are important in your context can be difficult, especially if you haven’t worked with a lot of smoke data. So two people can easily draw different conclusions from the same data.

As winemakers start to discuss the 2020 vintage, I have seen too many doomsday screeds about the wines that do not line up with the reality of the situation or the science.   2. I think that it is solid that Ridge accepts returns on the wine.
  3. I fully support those returning the wines and my view is not intended to suggest otherwise.

All that said, I think 2020 was a really tough year for some really good people that make something that is essential to my enjoyment of life. Frankly, there are several other board favorites that made 2020 wines that are no where near as good as their other vintages. Fires aside, it was a tough year otherwise. Are the wines, some of which exhibit some of the characteristics mentioned in this thread, smoke tainted or part of a bad vintage? posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: The full implication of your statement is that every bottle has to be tested, which of course is impossible.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
larry_schaffer:

You are assuming that there would be little to no meaningful variation ? See upstream for other takes on this wine . . .

If smoke taint was ‘clear’ to everyone, you would see the hat noted, but it does not appear to be so . . .

And again, it would be ‘instructional’ to compare and contrast versus other wines in the same region, no?

Bottle variation in currently used analytical markers isn’t an issue for smoke-impacted wines, at least in the first few years of their life (probably longer, I just haven’t seen data to confirm it). I can tell you this from personal experience, having seen multi-bottle analyses of at least a hundred different wines over the last few years.

Differences in personal experiences, as Adam points out, are much more likely due to differences in individuals. As for why an individual may have different experiences over several bottles, there are several possible reasons for that, but they wouldn’t show up in smoke analysis.

BillK:

Can anyone with $260 send a sample for testing? If so, how would one do that?

Anyone can submit any wine in their possession to a commercial lab for testing. I generally advise STRONGLY against this for advanced analytics like smoke. Sure, send it in for alcohol or pH or something if you’re curious. You could even send something in for malic acid if you’re wondering if the wine went through malolactic fermentation. But interpreting anything beyond basic chemistry is going to require more background knowledge than most people have. I used to work at two such commercial labs and it’s hard enough walking through the possible implications of the results with winemakers, let alone consumers analysing wines completely out of context.
